summary.brief: Butler v. Cayuga Med. Ctr.. Medical malpractice case involving neurology.

evidence_index.text: At around 1:00 p.m. on the afternoon of September 22, 2009, Ian Butler (hereinafter Butler), accompanied by his mother, Carol Butler (hereinafter decedent), sought treatment at Convenient Care at Ithaca (hereinafter CCI), an urgent care facility owned by defendant Cayuga Medical Center (hereinafter CMC).
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Defendant Eva Briggs, the physician who examined Butler at CCI, recommended that he be transported by ambulance to the CMC emergency department (hereinafter CMC-ED) to obtain a mental health evaluation and to complete certain blood work.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Although there is some discrepancy regarding timing, it is not disputed that Butler and decedent left the CMC-ED after they had waited for at least two hours.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Tragically, early in the morning of September 23, 2009, decedent was found dead in her home, and Butler was arrested for having caused her death.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Baum opined that Briggs timely and thoroughly examined Butler and reasonably determined that he needed a mental health evaluation and the specialized blood work — resources that were not available at CCI.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert testimony

evidence_index.text: Johnson opined that Briggs did not have a medical or legal basis to physically prevent Butler from leaving CCI to go to the CMC-ED.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert testimony

evidence_index.text: Katz opined that the medical care provided by Briggs, Koch and the triage staff at the CMC-ED 'deviated from accepted practice.'
evidence_index.speaker: Expert testimony

evidence_index.text: Having found that plaintiffs failed to raise a triable issue of fact, Supreme Court properly granted defendants' motions for summary judgment dismissing the complaints.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Liability will not arise from 'a mere error in professional judgment,' and where, as here, the alleged malpractice involves mental health treatment, 'it must be shown that the treatment decisions represented something less than a professional medical determination.'
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient had two grand mal seizures, complaining of blurred vision, headache, confusion. Mother states patient not compliant with anti-seizure meds.

focused_exam.general: Well-appearing, in no pain or distress, alert but tangential

reveal.clinical_significance: Needed to assess mental health status

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation at CCI
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with seizures and psychotic symptoms

key_events.event: Patient evaluated by nurse and Dr. Briggs

timeline_phases.name: Arrival at CMC-ED
timeline_phases.description: Patient arrives at CMC-ED for further evaluation

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient at CCI with acute psychosis symptoms
decision_points.prompt: Should the patient be transported by ambulance to the CMC-ED?

options.clinical_reasoning: Ensures safe arrival and timely evaluation

options.description: Allow patient to refuse ambulance transport
options.clinical_reasoning: Patient's autonomy respected, but risk not mitigated

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant allowed patient to refuse ambulance transport

expected_action_court.description: Patient should have been transported by ambulance
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence suggested need for safe transport

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to ensure safe transport led to delayed care
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Patient should have been transported by ambulance
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

decision_points.prompt: What triage level should be assigned to the patient?

options.clinical_reasoning: Less acute, based on available information

actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Based on patient's presentation and triage assessment

expected_action_court.description: Patient should have been assigned ESI level 2
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence suggested higher risk due to psychosis

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Patient's risk level underestimated, leading to delayed care
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Patient should have been prioritized for evaluation
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient's mother found dead, patient arrested

key_findings: No causation established between alleged malpractice and outcome

allegations.allegation: Failure to ensure safe transport and timely evaluation
